name: moss-tts-speech-foundation
description: Generates natural synthetic speech with token-level control over duration, pronunciation, and code-switching across languages. Deploy when you need controllable, multilingual speech synthesis with voice cloning without per-speaker training data.
paperTitle: MOSS-TTS Technical Report: Foundation Model for Speech Generation
keywords: [speech synthesis, text-to-speech, discrete audio tokens, multilingual, voice cloning]
content:
## Why This Matters

Speech synthesis has traditionally required either text-to-speech pipelines (simple but limited control) or large speaker-specific models (complex but high quality). MOSS-TTS bridges this gap by using discrete audio tokens (like language tokens) to enable token-level control, multilingual support, and voice cloning from minimal data—all in a single foundation model.

## Problem This Solves

- Standard TTS systems lack fine-grained control (duration, pronunciation, prosody)
- Voice cloning usually requires hours of speaker data per voice
- Code-switching (mixing languages in one utterance) produces unnatural artifacts
- Long-form generation often degrades in quality and stability
- Scaling across languages requires separate model training per language pair

## When to Use

Use MOSS-TTS when you need:
- Multilingual speech synthesis with natural prosody
- Token-level duration and pronunciation control
- Voice cloning from a few seconds of speaker audio
- Code-switching (e.g., English + Mandarin in one sentence)
- Long-form generation (minutes of speech) with stable quality
- Custom pronunciation control at phoneme/pinyin level

## When NOT to Use

Skip this approach when:
- Real-time synthesis is required (TTS typically runs offline)
- You need sub-100ms latency (model requires significant compute)
- Quality is less important than simplicity (rule-based TTS may suffice)
- Voices must match existing speakers perfectly (cloning introduces variation)

## Core Technique: Discrete Audio Tokenization + Autoregressive Generation

MOSS-TTS replaces continuous waveform generation with a three-stage pipeline:

### Stage 1: Audio Compression (Tokenizer)

Raw audio is compressed into discrete tokens using RVQ (Residual Vector Quantization):

```
Audio Waveform (16 kHz) → Causal Transformer → Token Codes (50 Hz)
```

This reduces audio to ~2–3% of the original size while preserving perceptual quality. Tokens are analogous to language tokens—discrete, learnable representations.

**Key benefit:** Discrete tokens enable:
- Easy concatenation and interpolation
- Token-level duration control
- Integration with text tokens in a unified vocabulary

### Stage 2: Semantic-Acoustic Unification

Text is first converted to phonemes (or pinyin for CJK), then to acoustic tokens via duration modeling:

```
Text → Phonemes → [duration_control] → Acoustic Tokens
```

The model learns alignments between phonemes and audio frames, enabling:
- Explicit phoneme-level pronunciation specification
- Duration override (stretch/compress individual phonemes)
- Controllable speech rate at sub-word granularity

### Stage 3: Autoregressive Decoding with Control Tokens

A causal Transformer decoder generates audio tokens token-by-token, conditioned on:
- Phoneme sequence
- Speaker embeddings (from voice cloning)
- Control codes (duration, pitch, emphasis)

```
[speaker_id] [phoneme_1:duration_ms] [phoneme_2:duration_ms] ... → [audio_token_1, audio_token_2, ...]
```

Each speaker embedding is learned from 3–10 seconds of reference audio.

## Implementation Outline

Here's a simplified version of the MOSS-TTS architecture:

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from typing import Dict, Tuple, Optional

class MOSSTokenizer(nn.Module):
    """
    Audio tokenizer using Causal Transformer + RVQ.
    Compresses raw audio to discrete codes.
    """

    def __init__(self, input_dim: int = 80, code_dim: int = 512, num_codes: int = 1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.input_dim = input_dim
        self.code_dim = code_dim
        self.num_codes = num_codes

        # Causal conv layers for feature extraction
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv1d(input_dim, 128, kernel_size=5, padding=2),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv1d(128, code_dim, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
        )

        # RVQ codebooks (hierarchical quantization)
        self.num_quantizers = 4
        self.codebooks = nn.ModuleList(
            [nn.Embedding(num_codes, code_dim) for _ in range(self.num_quantizers)]
        )

    def encode(self, mel_spectrogram: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Encode mel spectrogram to discrete codes.

        Args:
            mel_spectrogram: (B, T, mel_dim) mel-scale features

        Returns:
            codes: (B, T, num_quantizers) discrete token IDs
        """
        # Transpose for conv1d
        x = mel_spectrogram.transpose(1, 2)  # (B, mel_dim, T)

        # Encode to continuous representation
        z = self.encoder(x)  # (B, code_dim, T)
        z = z.transpose(1, 2)  # (B, T, code_dim)

        # Residual VQ (quantize progressively)
        codes = torch.zeros(z.shape[0], z.shape[1], self.num_quantizers, dtype=torch.long)
        residual = z

        for i, codebook in enumerate(self.codebooks):
            # Find nearest code
            distances = torch.cdist(residual, codebook.weight)  # (B, T, num_codes)
            codes[:, :, i] = distances.argmin(dim=-1)

            # Subtract quantized value from residual
            quantized = codebook(codes[:, :, i])  # (B, T, code_dim)
            residual = residual - quantized

        return codes


class MOSSSpeechDecoder(nn.Module):
    """
    Autoregressive decoder for speech token generation.
    Conditions on phonemes, speaker, and control codes.
    """

    def __init__(self, hidden_dim: int = 1024, num_audio_codes: int = 1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.num_audio_codes = num_audio_codes

        # Token embeddings
        self.phoneme_embed = nn.Embedding(150, hidden_dim)  # ~150 phonemes
        self.audio_embed = nn.Embedding(num_audio_codes, hidden_dim)
        self.speaker_embed = nn.Embedding(10000, hidden_dim // 4)

        # Causal transformer decoder
        decoder_layer = nn.TransformerDecoderLayer(
            d_model=hidden_dim,
            nhead=16,
            dim_feedforward=4096,
            batch_first=True
        )
        self.decoder = nn.TransformerDecoder(decoder_layer, num_layers=12)

        # Control token encoder (duration, pitch, emphasis)
        self.control_encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(3, 128),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, hidden_dim // 4)
        )

        # Output layer
        self.output_head = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, num_audio_codes)

    def forward(
        self,
        phoneme_ids: torch.Tensor,
        speaker_id: torch.Tensor,
        duration_ms: torch.Tensor,
        pitch: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None,
        prev_audio_codes: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Generate next audio token(s).

        Args:
            phoneme_ids: (B, seq_len) phoneme token indices
            speaker_id: (B,) speaker identifier
            duration_ms: (B, seq_len) target duration per phoneme in milliseconds
            pitch: (B, seq_len) optional pitch specification
            prev_audio_codes: (B, audio_seq_len) previously generated audio tokens

        Returns:
            logits: (B, num_audio_codes) distribution over next audio token
        """
        B, seq_len = phoneme_ids.shape

        # Embed phonemes
        phoneme_features = self.phoneme_embed(phoneme_ids)  # (B, seq_len, D)

        # Embed and broadcast speaker info
        speaker_features = self.speaker_embed(speaker_id)  # (B, D//4)
        speaker_features = speaker_features.unsqueeze(1).expand(B, seq_len, -1)  # (B, seq_len, D//4)

        # Encode control tokens (duration + pitch)
        control_input = torch.stack([
            duration_ms.float() / 100.0,  # Normalize duration
            pitch.mean(dim=1, keepdim=True).expand(-1, seq_len) if pitch is not None else torch.zeros_like(duration_ms.float()),
            torch.ones_like(duration_ms.float())  # Emphasis (default=1)
        ], dim=-1)  # (B, seq_len, 3)
        control_features = self.control_encoder(control_input)  # (B, seq_len, D//4)

        # Concatenate all features
        combined = torch.cat([phoneme_features, speaker_features, control_features], dim=-1)  # (B, seq_len, D)

        # Autoregressive decoding with previous audio context
        if prev_audio_codes is not None:
            audio_features = self.audio_embed(prev_audio_codes)  # (B, audio_len, D)
            # Append to phoneme context
            combined = torch.cat([audio_features, combined], dim=1)

        # Causal transformer
        decoder_out = self.decoder(combined, combined)  # (B, seq_len, D)

        # Predict next audio tokens
        logits = self.output_head(decoder_out[:, -1, :])  # (B, num_codes)

        return logits
```

This shows the two key components:
- **Tokenizer:** Compresses audio to discrete codes via RVQ
- **Decoder:** Generates audio tokens from phonemes with explicit control

## Architecture & Components

**Pipeline Flow:**
```
Input Text
    ↓
Phoneme Conversion (linguistic front-end)
    ↓
Duration Prediction / Override
    ↓
Speaker Embedding (from voice cloning audio)
    ↓
Causal Transformer Decoder
    ↓
Audio Token Sequence
    ↓
Vocoder Reconstruction (inverse tokenizer)
    ↓
Waveform Output
```

**Key Design Choices:**
- **Causal convolutions in tokenizer** ensure future audio doesn't leak to past tokens
- **RVQ (4 quantizers)** provides progressive refinement without separate training per level
- **Unified token vocabulary** allows phonemes and audio tokens to interact (enables better alignment)
- **Speaker embeddings** learned from 3–10 sec of reference audio (minimal data per voice)

## Hyperparameters & Tuning

| Parameter | Default | Range | Effect |
|-----------|---------|-------|--------|
| num_quantizers | 4 | 2–8 | Fewer = faster, lower quality; More = slower, better quality |
| code_dim | 512 | 256–1024 | Larger = expressive but slow |
| decoder_depth | 12 | 8–16 | Deeper = better quality, higher latency |
| num_codes | 1024 | 512–4096 | Fewer = quantization loss; More = overfitting risk |
| speaker_embed_dim | 256 | 128–512 | Larger = finer voice variation |
| duration_scale | 1.0 | 0.5–2.0 | <1.0 = faster speech; >1.0 = slower |

**Tuning for different use cases:**

*Low-latency synthesis (online streaming):*
- Reduce num_quantizers to 2
- Decrease decoder_depth to 8
- Use shorter phoneme context windows

*High-quality offline synthesis:*
- Increase num_quantizers to 6–8
- Use decoder_depth = 16
- Longer conditioning windows (full sentence)

*Voice cloning with limited data:*
- Use speaker_embed_dim = 512 (capture nuance)
- Fine-tune on 10–30 sec of reference audio
- Regularize speaker embeddings to prevent overfitting

## Control Interface

Users interact with MOSS-TTS via control codes:

```python
# Example: Custom pronunciation
text = "Anthropic"
phonemes = ["ae", "n", "th", "r", "oh", "p", "ih", "k"]
durations_ms = [80, 60, 100, 80, 100, 80, 80, 80]  # Stretch "o"
pitch = None  # Use default prosody

audio = synthesize(text, phonemes, durations_ms, speaker_id=42, pitch=pitch)

# Example: Code-switching (English + Mandarin)
text = "Hello 世界"  # "Hello world" in mixed code
phonemes_en = ["h", "ax", "l", "oh"]
phonemes_zh = ["sh", "ij", "j", "ie"]
combined_phonemes = phonemes_en + phonemes_zh

audio = synthesize_codeswitched(combined_phonemes, speaker_id=42)
```

## Integration Notes

**Input Requirements:**
- Text (UTF-8, any language)
- Speaker ID or reference audio (3–10 sec) for voice cloning
- Optional: phoneme overrides, duration adjustments, pitch specifications

**Output Format:**
- 16 kHz mono waveform
- Quality comparable to professional TTS (MOS ~4.3–4.5 on 5-point scale)
- Inference time: ~10–50x real-time (depending on hardware)

**Training Data:**
- Multilingual speech corpus (English, Mandarin, Spanish, etc.)
- Duration alignments (phoneme-to-frame)
- Speaker diversity (100+ speakers recommended)

## Related Approaches

- **FastPitch + Vocoder:** Faster but less flexible control
- **Glow-TTS:** Normalizing flows; good quality but complex
- **VQ-VAE + NeuroVPS:** Similar discrete approach; may require more computation
- **Streaming TTS:** Real-time synthesis; trade quality for speed

## References

- MOSS-TTS paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18090
- RVQ (Residual Vector Quantization): Défossez et al. (2022), "High Fidelity Neural Audio Compression"
- Voice conversion via speaker embeddings: Toda et al. (2016), "Voice Conversion Synthesis from Parallel Non-Aligned Corpora Using an Adaptive Gaussian Mixture Model"
- FastSpeech (duration modeling): Ren et al. (2020), "FastSpeech: Fast, Robust and Controllable Text-to-Speech"
